assumptions: Git and Heroku CLI can be installed.

developer_goal: Create a Heroku account, deploy the official Node.js sample, and open the app URL.

first_success.outcome: The Node.js sample is running on a Heroku dyno and loads at its herokuapp.com URL.
first_success.observable_signal: Browser shows the sample app content after heroku open or visiting the app URL.

route_selection.surface: Getting Started on Heroku with Node.js sample deploy
route_selection.rule: Measure documented developer onboarding: account creation through first success. Prefer the vendor quickstart or hosted API/console path when one exists.
route_selection.selected: Getting Started on Heroku with Node.js sample deploy

candidates.name: Getting Started on Heroku with Node.js sample deploy
candidates.reason: Heroku Dev Center Getting Started with Node.js is the general Cedar sample deploy: CLI login, clone official sample, heroku create, git push heroku main, open the herokuapp.com URL. Language peers exist; Node.js is a complete official path.

required_path.action: Install the Heroku CLI and Git.
Run heroku login and complete browser authentication.
Run heroku create --stack heroku-26 to create the app and git remote.
required_path.observable_result: App URL and git.heroku.com remote printed.

required_path.action: Deploy with git push heroku main (or master as documented).
Open the app with heroku open and confirm the sample site.

prerequisites.description: Dyno usage may incur charges; tutorial recommends low-cost plans.

sources.title: Getting Started on Heroku with Node.js

uncertainties.question: Are all signup and first-success fields for Heroku fully enumerated in public docs?
uncertainties.impact: Atomic field counts inside account or billing steps may be incomplete.
uncertainties.evidence_needed: Live console fields can differ from documentation screenshots.
